coredata.dc:description: In this work, a series of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized with high photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) and outstanding thermal stability. With the reaction solvent changing from water, ethanol, methanol, and dimethylformamide (DMF), a significant fluorescence emission with PLQY improvement from 42 %, 33 %, 25 %, and 18 %, respectively, accompanied by a red shift from 430 to 590 nm, (the color changes from blue, green, yellow, and red respectively). Simultaneously, excellent fluorescence stability can also be obtained across temperatures ranging from 15 to 95 °C. Combined with the density functional calculations (DFT) results, the underlying mechanism investigation reveals that the color change of fluorescence emission was probably induced by the increased particle size of CDs and increased graphitic N content. The enhanced thermal stability is induced by the presence of stable surface functional groups, including C=O, C=N, C=S, and -NH, among others, contributing to improved hydrophilicity, regulated particle aggregation, and mitigated thermal oxidation by limiting oxygen diffusion to fluorescent hubs. Notably, the obtained outstanding optical properties finally render these multicolor CDs suitable for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and sulfur Co-doped carbon dots with excellent fluorescent thermal stability for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a series of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized with high photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) and outstanding thermal stability. With the reaction solvent changing from water, ethanol, methanol, and dimethylformamide (DMF), a significant fluorescence emission with PLQY improvement from 42 %, 33 %, 25 %, and 18 %, respectively, accompanied by a red shift from 430 to 590 nm, (the color changes from blue, green, yellow, and red respectively). Simultaneously, excellent fluorescence stability can also be obtained across temperatures ranging from 15 to 95 °C. Combined with the density functional calculations (DFT) results, the underlying mechanism investigation reveals that the color change of fluorescence emission was probably induced by the increased particle size of CDs and increased graphitic N content. The enhanced thermal stability is induced by the presence of stable surface functional groups, including C=O, C=N, C=S, and -NH, among others, contributing to improved hydrophilicity, regulated particle aggregation, and mitigated thermal oxidation by limiting oxygen diffusion to fluorescent hubs. Notably, the obtained outstanding optical properties finally render these multicolor CDs suitable for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dot

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Widespread counterfeiting poses significant challenges in various domains, including the replication of goods, official documents, passports, barcodes, and currency. These illicit activities pose a substantial threat to industries, governments, and consumers. The implementation of robust anticounterfeiting measures has become imperative for safeguarding product integrity, ensuring document security, and maintaining trust in financial transactions [1,2]. Various luminescent materials have been studied for anti-counterfeiting applications such as inorganic semiconductors, organic dyes, lanthanide-doped nanoparticles, and transition-metal complexes [3–5]. However, several drawbacks including, high synthesis costs, high complexity, potential toxicity, and low sustainability, greatly limited their large-area applications. As a result, CDs have been regarded as promising substitutes that can be capable of providing effective encryption abilities with strong luminescent output due to their notable chemical stability and excellent photostability [6–8]. For example, Zhang W et al. synthesized multicolor CDs for anti-counterfeiting applications with suitable chemical stability, exhibiting only 15−28 % fluorescence quenching after 5 months of storage and treatment with HCl and NaOH [9] Jia. et al. prepared multicolor CDs with excitation-independent emission for anticounterfeiting applications, showing that the PL intensity decreased mildly after continuous xenon arc light irradiation at 500 W for 3 h [10]. Recently, Cheng et al. fabricated carbon dot@silica composite, with an outstanding property of anti-photobleaching with continuous irradiation with UV light 365 nm, 6 W for 12 h, resulting in minor variations of fluorescence intensity and applied it for anti-counterfeiting applications [11]. Although the outstanding performance has been noted, the fluorescence emission intensity was always significantly quenched by ≥ 25 % at higher temperatures for most of the previously reported CDs, as a result of sufficient non-radiative channels and particle aggregation [12,13].

At present, only limited studies focused on the fluorescence thermal stability of CDs. For example, a green-emitting CD with an average size of 1.9 nm was reported, exhibiting excellent thermal stability under harsh conditions of 10 °C–80 °C and PLQY of 10.1 % [14]. Exceptional thermal stability for yellow-emitting CDs (PLQY: 0.56 %) was found, with minor changes in fluorescence intensity observed at 100 °C over 12 days [15]. However, due to their limited PLQY, these thermally stable CDs are hardly suitable for anti-counterfeiting applications. As reported, nitrogen (N) atom doping is an effective strategy for improving PLQY, due to its ability to form amine functional groups on the surface of CDs [16–18]. The presence of amino groups on the surface of CDs leads to a higher degree of connectivity among conjugated systems, which promotes electron transitions from the ground state to the lowest excited singlet state, resulting in a higher PLQY [19]. For example, Hu et al. observed that the presence of electron-donating amino groups on N-CDs surface led to an increase in PLQY from 8.7 % to 24.6 %, attributed to the radiative recombination of surface-trapped electrons and holes [20]. Song et al. reported a remarkable PLQY of 85.84 %, which was attributed to the presence of nitrogen-containing molecular fluorophores [21] Jiang et al. found that by increasing N content from 3.6 % to 15.5 %, the quantum yield was improved from 4 % to 21 % [22]. Additionally, the element sulfur (S), as another dopant for CDs, also shows a great ability to improve flouresence stability and increase PLQY, due to the suppression of non-radiative transitions caused by passivating surface defects and reducing trap densities [23]. For instance, compared to that without the doped S element, Li et al. demonstrated that the S-doped CDs exhibited the greenish-yellow PL emission in dispersed and powder states with the increased PLQY up to 32 %, attributed to the larger steric hindrance, a flatter structure and deteriorated intramolecular motion or π-π stacking [24]. Benefting from the graphitic nitrogen content increasing from 5.7 % to 59.5 % that contributed by doped S element, which led to the transformation of internal structure and surface group, Gao et al. observed that the fluorescence emission of S doped CDs become yellow and PLQY increased from 22 % to 26 %, along with the superior structure stability that can be applied in various applications [25]. Inspired by these results, it can be expected that co-doping with N and S can effectively adjust the optical characteristics and chemical structure of CDs, resulting in simultaneously enhanced PLQY and stability [26].

In this study, a series of multicolor N, S co-doped CDs with high PLQY and exceptional thermal stability were synthesized by utilizing citric acid and thiourea as precursors. By switching the reaction solvent from water to DMF, the fluorescence emission of the prepared CDs underwent a red shift from 430 to 590 nm, and a significant improvement in PLQYs from 42 %, 33 %, 25 %, and 18 %. The DFT calculations and experimental results support the attribution of band gap variations to an increase in particle size resulting from sp2 conjugated domains and higher graphitic N content. Moreover, their exceptional thermal stability up to 95 °C is attributed to stable surface functional groups like C=O, C=N, C=S, and -NH, which regulate particle aggregation, and limit thermal oxidation by restricting oxygen diffusion to fluorescent centres. Finally, the synthesized CDs with excellent thermal stability, higher quantum yields, and multiple colors ranging from blue to red are applicable in invisible fluorescent ink for anticounterfeiting and information encryption.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the multicolor CDs with blue, green, yellow, and red fluorescence emissions were synthesized by heating a mixture of citric acid as a source of carbon and thiourea acting as a source of N and S in four different solvents (water, ethanol, methanol, and N, N-Dimethylformamide DMF). During the solvothermal reaction, dehydration reactions occurred between citric acid and thiourea, leading to the formation of nitrogen and sulfur-rich groups, including carboxyl, carbonyl, amine, thiol, and hydroxyl, respectively. The selection of the reaction solvent significantly impacted the degree of dehydration and carbonization occurring between citric acid and thiourea. In an aqueous solution, the high polarity and broad dissolution range enabled the creation of numerous hydroxy and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs through hydrogen bonding [27]. However, the relatively moderate boiling point of water restricted the dehydration reactions among carbon sources, leading to the formation of fewer sp2 conjugated domains and resulting in blue color emission. Hydrocarbon solvents such as ethanol and methanol, with lower boiling points and lower polarity, provided a favorable environment for dehydration and carbonization, promoting the formation of larger sp2 conjugated domains and resulting in green and yellow emissions [28]. DMF, being an aprotic solvent, also facilitated higher dehydration compared to protic solvents like water, however, the higher nitrogen content in DMF played a vital role in producing red emission [29].

Notably, during the solvothermal process, these formed domains acted as nucleation sites for CDs particle growth, influencing their size, morphology, and optical properties. Through these observations, we successfully developed multicolor emissive CDs by simply varying the reaction solvents. Comprehensive optimization was conducted to achieve multicolor emission, with detailed experimental information available in S1 and Table S1 supporting information. Based on their color emissions, four typical samples were labeled as B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs.

To show the successful formation of CDs, the sizes and morphologies were analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2(a)–(d), (g), and (j) , for the four target samples, TEM images reveal the spherical, monodisperse particles with good dispersion. In order of the B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs, a gradual increase in the particle size was observed with average size of 2.7 ± 0.5 nm, 3.2 ± 0.6 nm, 3.9 ± 0.7 nm, and 5.2 ± 0.8 nm, respectively. To support the observed increase in particle size, DLS measurements were conducted for all samples. The results confirm an increase in particle size from 2.7 nm in B-CDs to 6.0 nm in R-CDs, as presented in Fig. S14. This observation confirms the expansion of sp2-conjugated domains [30]. In high-resolution, transmission electron microscopy images (Fig. 2(b, h, e), and k), a clear crystalline lattice of 0.22 nm is revealed by the (002) diffraction facets, indicating (100) graphitic carbon. To explain their degree of crystallization and disorder, Raman spectra of all samples were examined, and two distinct peaks were exhibited at 1428 and 1679 cm⁻1, corresponding to the D and G bands, respectively (Fig. 3 (f)). As we know, the D band typically indicates structural disorder and defects, whereas the G band signifies the presence of a well-organized sp2 carbon network, and two distinct peaks are exhibited at 1428 and 1679 cm⁻1, corresponding to the D and G bands, respectively [31–34]. As we know, the D band typically indicates structural disorder and defects due to heteroatom doping, whereas the G band signifies the presence of a well-organized sp2 carbon network. Herein, a progressive reduction in structural defects can be noted across the B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs, reflected in ID/IG values of 1.26, 1.12, 1.14, and 1.06 respectively. Moreover, a detailed analysis of the FTIR spectra for all the samples was collected to investigate the crucial role of functional groups and chemical bonds. As depicted in Fig. 3(a–d), for all samples, the distinct stretching vibrations can be observed at approximately 3434 and 2939 cm⁻1 corresponding to O─H/N─H and C─H, functional groups, respectively, which will be responsible for the hydrophilicity and stability. Other peaks at 1447 cm⁻1, 1687 cm⁻1, 1055 cm⁻1, and 929 cm⁻1 represent stretching vibrations of C=N, C=O, O─H, and C─S groups, respectively [35,36] Notably, the peaks associated with C=O and O─H show a gradual shift in intensity, indicating that G-CDs and Y-CDs may possess a higher concentration of C=O groups and an enhanced degree of conjugation [37,38]. Additionally, the higher intensity of the stretching vibration at 1447 cm⁻1 in R-CDs is attributed to the dominance of graphitic nitrogen due to DMF's role as both a nitrogen dopant source and solvent.

To further validate the elemental composition and heteroatom doping, XPS was conducted. Fig. 4 (a) shows full XPS spectra, providing evidence of the effective co-doping of S and N in four synthesized CDs. In Fig. 4(c)–(f), the high-resolution XPS spectra have been carefully fitted. For the C1s spectra, three distinct peaks are observed at 284.5, 285.8, and 288.5 eV, which correspond to sp2 hybridized carbon atoms (C=C), sp³ hybridized carbon atoms in C-S/C-N/C-O environments, and carbonyl groups (C=O), respectively [39,40]. In the N1s band, three peaks at 398.19, 400.40, and 403.84 eV represent pyrolic N, graphitic N, and oxidized nitrogen species (N=O/N-OH), respectively [41,42]. Based on the elemental composition analysis illustrated in Fig. 4(b) and Table S4, the N content increased from B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs with values of 4.93 %, 16.1 %, 14.23 %, and 18.31 % respectively. The increased N content in R-CDs could be ascribed to the dual functionality of DMF, serving as both a solvent and an N source during the solvothermal process.

This allows for the integration of higher N from both thiourea and DMF into the carbon framework [43]. Notably, the high-resolution N 1s data shows that graphitic N and pyrrolic N are present in higher concentrations as compared to pyridinic N. A higher concentration of graphitic N can modify the optical characteristics by contributing an extra electron to the vacant π∗ orbitals of the sp2 carbon lattice and creating an intermediate energy gap between the HOMO and LUMO [44,45]. Density functional calculations strongly support the assertion that the substantial redshift observed in R-CDs is due to a lower HOMO-LUMO gap resulting from the presence of higher graphitic N content, as illustrated in Fig. 8. However, pyrrolic nitrogen, due to its formation of σ-bonds with neighboring carbon and hydrogen atoms, has minimal impact on the HOMO-LUMO gap, as there are no additional electrons available for higher-energy electronic states [46]. The O1s spectrum, peaks at 531 and 532 eV correspond to C=O and C–O, respectively (Fig. 4(e)). The C=O group exhibits similar peak intensities in B-CDs, G-CDs, and Y-CDs, whereas R-CDs show lower intensity, indicating varying content of the C=O group on the surface. The S 2p fitted bands can be divided into peaks at ∼164, 168, and 169 eV corresponding to S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 representing C–S, S=O, and C–SO2 sulfone bridges, respectively (Fig. 4(f)). The absence of C-S bonds in B-CDs, G-CDs, and Y-CDs can be attributed to the use of protic solvents (water, ethanol, methanol), which favor the formation of other sulfur-containing groups. In contrast, DMF, a polar aprotic solvent, enhances sulfur incorporation, leading to detectable C-S bonds in R-CDs. A notable reduction in the elemental composition of sulfur content was observed, decreasing from 19.09 %, 17.04 %, 15.24 %, to 8.26 % in B -CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs, respectively. The higher sulfur content in B-CDs may disrupt the ordered sp2 carbon cluster, potentially promoting the development of disordered structural configurations [47,48].

The UV–visible absorption spectra were utilized to unveil the optical properties of the synthesized CDs, which arise from their intrinsic electronic energy levels. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), B-CDs exhibit three significant absorption bands at 220, 235, and 250 nm, respectively, origins of these absorption peaks can be traced to specific molecular π-π∗ transition of aromatic sp2 conjugated domain [49]. Similarly, G-CDs show absorption bands at 220 and 260 nm, however, Y-CDs exhibit significant absorption peaks at 310 nm corresponding to strong aromatic sp2 conjugated domain (Fig. 5(b) and c). The absorption intensity increases gradually from B-CDs to Y-CDs, indicating enhanced conjugation attributed to a uniform electron cloud density across the CDs and greater electron delocalization. This phenomenon reduces the energy required for electronic transitions, leading to a narrowing of the energy band gap, as confirmed by our theoretical calculations (Fig. 8). Additionally, R-CDs demonstrate significant absorption bands at 335, 520, and 555 nm, indicating well-resolved n−π∗ transitions of C=O, C=S, and C=N on the surface of R-CDs (Fig. 5(d)) [50]. These multiple absorption bands align with the PL excitation spectrum.

As depicted in Fig. 5(a)–(d), B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs exhibit maximum emission peaks at 430, 490, 510, and 600 nm, respectively, with excitation wavelengths of 365, 380, 410, and 470 nm, respectively. The excitation spectra of B-CDs and G-CDs are narrow, typically falling within the UV region, while those of Y-CDs and R-CDs extend from UV to the visible region. Additionally, B-CDs and R-CDs display an excitation-dependent behavior, with a maximum emission peak shifting from 367 to 394 nm, and from 603 to 623 nm, resulting in a 27 and 23 nm red shift, respectively (Fig. S3). These observed redshifts are likely due to the synergistic effects of S and N co-doping, potentially impacting charge carrier mobility and recombination rates [51]. Additionally, the multicolor CDs have been shown to improve PLQY. Using quinine sulfate as a standard, the PLQY values for B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs were found to be 42 %, 33 %, 25 %, and 18 %, respectively [52]. For the B-CDs, the observed highest PLQY can be attributed to the higher S content. As mentioned in previous studies, the higher presence of sulfur atoms can interfere with the chemical bond between nitrogen and carbon atoms. This results in the optoelectronic properties of CDs becoming less reliant on the energy of absorbed light [26]. To further investigate the role of sulfur, we synthesized eight additional CDs. One set was prepared without sulfur, using urea as the nitrogen source in four different solvents: water, ethanol, methanol, and DMF, labeled as NCDs-I, NCDs-II, NCDs-III, and NCDs-IV (Fig. S13). The other set consisted of oxygen-containing CDs (O-CDs) prepared with citric acid under the same conditions in the same solvents, labeled as OCDs-I, OCDs-II, OCDs-III, and OCDs-IV. The optical properties of these nitrogen-doped CDs differed markedly from those of the N, S-CDs as shown in Figs. S8 and S9. Both NCDs and OCDs exhibited significantly lower PLQY compared to the N, S-CDs (Figs. S13(b) and (c)). Based on these results we can conclude that the high PLQY can be attributed to the synergetic effect of N and S which boosts the performance. Meanwhile, the inclusion of S atoms introduces new energy levels, thereby modifying the energy band structure of CDs. Notably, this work achieved an improvement in PLQY compared to other reported CDs, as presented in Table S2. Furthermore, to understand the photophysical PL dynamic process, the PL time-resolved decay for all samples was determined. Generally, the presence of multiple exponential components allows for the calculation of the average decay lifetime for all samples. The decay in fluorescence intensity provides insights into the deactivation of electrons from the ground state to the excited state through both radiative and non-radiative pathways [53]. The lifetime components,

, are attributed to recombination processes occurring in the core states and surface states, respectively [54]. As shown in Fig. 6 (a)–(d), the average lifetime of all samples from B-CDs to R-CDs decreases by 12.66, 11.09, 5.99, and 4.43 ns respectively. As shown in Table S3, the Kr values surpass Knr by approximately 1.5 times across B-CDs, G-CDs, and Y-CDs, emphasizing the dominance of radiative transitions. However, in the case of R-CDs, the non-radiative transitions have higher values, thereby restraining the contribution of radiative transitions. This disparity may account for the lower PLQY observed in R-CDs compared to B-CDs, G-CDs, and Y-CDs.

Fluorescence thermal stability is crucial for CDs to withstand harsh environmental conditions in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption applications. The stability performance was investigated under three different conditions: (1) heating test in a wide temperature range from 15 °C to 95 °C (2) heating test at a fixed temperature for 180 min; (3) exposure to a certain radiant intensity of UV illumination (20 mW/cm2) from a 250 W mercury lamp for varying durations for 180 min. The resulting evolution fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs aqueous solutions are given in Figs. S10 and S11. The PL thermal stabilities of B-CDs to R-CDs were investigated over a temperature range of 15 °C–95 °C with corresponding PL spectra, as depicted in Fig. 7 (a)–(d). Remarkably, all samples from B-CDs to R-CDs demonstrated exceptional stability with only a slight 2–5% decrease in the PL intensity. Across the temperature range of 15 °C–95 °C, no significant fluctuations in PL intensity were observed among the four synthesized CDs. As shown in Fig. S10, the N,S-CDs from B-CDs to R-CDs exhibit exceptional thermal stability across a range of temperatures. When the temperature was increased from 15 °C to 60 °C under ambient air, N,S-CDs almost retained their initial fluorescence intensity. Interestingly, when the temperature was further raised to 70 °C and 90 °C, a slight enhancement of ≈8 % in fluorescence intensity was observed. To further investigate their thermal stability, the N,S-CDs were subjected to prolonged heating at fixed temperatures of 60 °C, 75 °C, and 90 °C (Fig. S10). Throughout the heating process, slight fluorescence decay was observed at these temperature points. Instead, after 180 min of heating, the N,S-CDs showed a quenching of fluorescence intensity enhancement of 10 % for B-CDs, 7 % for G-CDs, 5 % for Y-CDs and 3 % for RCDs, respectively, confirming their excellent thermal resilience. Additionally, the photo-stability of the N-CDs was also evaluated by exposing them to UV illumination (20 mW/cm2) from a 250 W mercury lamp for varying durations (Fig. S11). The results indicated that the fluorescence intensity remained stable, even after prolonged exposure. After 180 min of UV exposure, the N, S-CDs maintained 90 % of their initial fluorescence intensity, demonstrating outstanding photostability as shown in Fig. S11 in the supporting information. Moreover, the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs reveals multi-step thermal degradation patterns, indicating differences in composition and thermal behavior. As shown in Fig. S12 all samples experience significant mass loss in the temperature range (180–202 °C), primarily due to the solvents and volatile organic compounds. Intermediate degradation phases occur around 253 °C for all CDs, corresponding to the breakdown of organic components, while final degradation stages, occurring between 286 °C and 430 °C, are linked to the decomposition of more thermally stable carbonaceous structures. These results indicate that the thermal stability of the CDs is strongly influenced by the solvent used during synthesis, with B-CDs and R-CDs showing moderate thermal resistance, while G-CDs and Y-CDs display lower stability at moderate temperatures. These suggest that the obtained samples are highly stable, which can be attributed to the stable composition of surface states, i.e., C=O, O-H, -NH, and C=S groups that were discussed in XPS and FTIR results. As documented in the previous reports, the N and O states can regulate the accumulation of CDs at higher temperatures, decreasing the diffusion intensity of oxygen to the fluorescent hubs and preventing thermal oxidation [56]. Although most previously reported CDs exhibited thermal quenching at such high temperatures, the synthesized samples in this work show great chemical and optical stability against the temperature.

The fluorescence mechanisms of CDs remain a prominent area of research, with several theories proposed to elucidate this phenomenon, including the quantum confinement effect, quantum size effect, surface state, crosslink-enhanced emission, and molecular state [57–60]. However, in this study, we found that both the quantum size effect and graphitic N are dominant factors for controlling the fluorescence emission for synthesized CDs. Our observations emphasize the importance of heteroatom co-doping and the choice of solvent in determining multicolor emission, spanning from B-CDs to R-CDs. The synergetic effect of nitrogen and sulfur co-doping enhances both the PLQY and thermal stability of CDs by suppressing non-radiative transitions, improving surface passivation, and reducing the influence of oxygen-related states. Consequently, N, S-CDs exhibit significantly higher fluorescence efficiency and thermal resilience compared to N-CDs and O-CDs, demonstrating the critical role of sulfur in optimizing optoelectronic performance (Fig. S13). Meanwhile, TEM and DLS results confirm that the increase in particle size for B-CDs, G-CDs, and Y-CDs is due to a larger sp2-conjugated domain, while R-CDs emission was attributed to higher content of graphitic N as confirmed by XPS results. The enhanced conjugation observed from B-CDs to Y-CDs can be ascribed to the physicochemical properties of the reaction solvents. These properties induce dehydration and carbonization thereby promoting the development of larger sp2-conjugated domains that lead to a decrease in the bandgap [61]. These domains serve as nucleation sites for the growth of particles in CDs, influencing their size. However, the higher content of graphitic N in R-CDs results in an intermediate energy gap between the HOMO and LUMO, subsequently causing a redshift in fluorescence emission. A greater π-electron system or an increased graphitic nitrogen content leads to a smaller energy gap, causing the photoluminescence emission to shift towards the red region. Additionally, the luminescent centres, where fluorescence is emitted through the recombination of electrons and holes, are strongly influenced by the level of π-electron conjugation and the presence of graphitic nitrogen [40].

To verify the above results density functional theory simulations were conducted to simulate CDs. Initially, geometry optimization was carried out using GAMESS with the M06/6-31G∗ theory of computations [62,63]. Energy calculations were performed in G09 with 6-31G′(d,p) basis set and M06 functional to evaluate the solvent effect and heteroatom co-doping effect [64]. As shown in Fig. 8(a), changing the reaction solvents from water, ethanol, and methanol the energy band gap decreases from 1.74, 1.73, and 1.61 eV respectively. This indicates LUMO and HOMO narrow as the particle size increases due to expanding sp2-conjugated domains in B-CDs, G-CDs, and Y-CDs systems. Conversely, the R-CDs exhibit a decrease in band gap attributed to the elevated graphitic N content. Previous reports confirm that the graphitic N generates deep gap states which reduce the energy of lower optical transitions [65]. Graphitic N can alter optical properties by injecting excessive electrons into π∗ orbitals of the sp2 carbon lattice. Nevertheless, despite the higher concentration of pyrrolic N in all samples, it does not make a significant contribution to altering the band gap, as it lacks sufficient electrons to occupy higher electronic states. Because pyrrolic N atoms can form three σ bonds with neighboring carbon and hydrogen atoms, using three of their five valence electrons [66]. The remaining electrons pair satisfy the aromatic sextet rule, therefore no “extra” electrons are available to occupy the higher-lying electronic state [67]. Therefore, pyrrolic N does not significantly alter the HOMO-LUMO by introducing new states. Based on these findings the suggested mechanism for band gap transitions of multicolor CDs from B-CDs to R-CDs can be shown in Fig. 8(b). These findings offer valuable insights into the mechanisms that govern the luminescence of CDs, considering the effects of solvent and heteroatom doping, and hold significant implications for their potential applications.

Based on the high PLQY and remarkable thermal stability of prepared CDs, they were applied for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption applications. Fig. 9 (a)–(d) illustrates typical patterns featuring an eagle in a hunting pose. Notably, B-CDs and G-CDs exhibit a high 97 % invisibility, while Y-CDs and R-CDs show a slightly lower 70 % invisibility. These invisible patterns can serve as unique identifiers or authentication marks that are difficult to replicate [68,69]. Further utilization of multicolor CDs in various anti-counterfeiting applications including fingerprints, QR codes, and logos illustrated in Fig. S4. Beyond visual characteristics, the practical stability of all synthesized samples at elevated temperatures from 75 °C to 95 °C is shown in Fig. 9(e)–(h). Importantly, the multicolor CDs in anti-counterfeiting applications remain invisible, consistent, and reproducible for an extended duration of more than 10 months showing outstanding stable performance (Fig. S6). Additionally, a data pattern was generated by utilizing multicolor CDs, incorporating a 27-alphabet character set (Fig. S5). Most characters are imperceptible under typical daylight conditions, with Y-CDs and R-CDs being faintly visible during daylight and B-CDs and G-CDs exhibited invisibility. Intense red LED light erases characters, and lower intensity renders them faintly visible. Under 365 nm UV light, the entire dataset becomes sharply visible, highlighting the potential of utilizing multicolor CDs for information encryption. Furthermore, B-CD was incorporated into PVA, as shown in Fig. S7. The fluorescent film formed by B-CD/PVA is transparent under daylight and exhibits blue emission under UV illumination. This composite demonstrates versatility in adopting different geometric configurations and possesses exceptional qualities such as flexibility, durability, and prolonged emission stability, making it well-suited for various applications. B-CDs/PVA composites may extend beyond packaging applications, offering advanced anti-counterfeiting solutions for packing luxury items such as diamonds, jewellery, and expensive watches.

In conclusion, the multicolor N and S co-doped CDs with B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs have been developed by the one-step solvothermal method. Variation of the reaction solvents for these multicolor CDs shows a significant redshift in the PL emission peak from 420 to 590 nm with a large improvement in the quantum yield from 42 %, 33 %, 25 %, and 18 %. The redshift is likely attributed to the enlargement of particle size resulting from dehydration and carbonization of precursor materials. This process leads to an increase in the number of sp2-conjugated domains and elevated graphitic N content, consequently decreasing the energy band gap values from B-CDs to R-CDs. Furthermore, these multicolor CDs exhibit excellent thermal stability at different temperatures ranging from 15 to 95 °C. All these features make them potential candidates for further application of fluorescent ink for loading and sharing secret information and anti-counterfeiting applications. Moreover, integrating these B-CDs with PVA yields composite films with remarkable flexibility, high transparency, and durability, which is promising for item packing and advanced anti-counterfeiting. In the future, we envision that this study will pave the way for developing multicolor emissions with exceptional thermal stability and high PLQY thereby expanding the application possibilities of CDs.
